content.text: Azure Availability Zones are physically separate datacentres within a region. When you deploy a resource in a region that supports AZs, it falls into one of three zone models depending on the service type and how you configure it:

the resource is explicitly placed in a specific AZ (Zone 1, 2, or 3). It lives in exactly one zone and fails if that zone fails. You control placement — and you are responsible for spreading related resources across zones yourself.
the platform automatically distributes the resource across all AZs. If one zone fails, the remaining zones continue serving. No customer action is required during a zone failure.
the resource has no zone affinity. The platform places it somewhere in the region and may or may not move it across zones over time. No zone-level SLA guarantee applies.

attrs.alt: Zonal vs Zone-Redundant vs Non-Zonal Services: three zones in a region, showing zonal VMs pinned to zones 1 and 3, a zone-redundant Standard Load Balancer spanning all three, ZRS storage with one copy per zone, and a non-zonal App Service with no zone assignment

content.text: Zone-redundant services survive a zone failure automatically. Zonal services survive only if you spread replicas across zones — pinning all replicas to Zone 1 provides no zone-level protection.
Zonal Services: What They Are and When to Use Them

A zonal service is one where you explicitly pin the resource to a specific Availability Zone. The Azure control plane places the resource's underlying hardware in that zone and guarantees it stays there for the resource's lifetime.
Primary Use Cases for Zone Pinning

a database server and its attached managed disk must be in the same zone. Managed disks are zonal — they can only be mounted by VMs in the same zone. If you zone-pin the VM, the disk is automatically placed in the same zone.

services that call each other thousands of times per second benefit from being in the same zone. Cross-zone traffic within a region has ~2 ms additional latency vs intra-zone traffic. For most applications this is irrelevant; for ultra-low-latency trading systems or high-frequency control planes, it matters.

when you want to guarantee that instances are spread across exactly the failure domains you choose (e.g., one instance per zone, no two instances in the same zone), zonal pinning provides that control.
VM and its OS disk placed in Zone 1
Must be in same zone as attached VM
Each node in the pool placed in that zone
Standard IPs can be zonal or zone-redundant
Inherits zone of attached Public IP

# Deploy a VM pinned to Zone 1 in East US
az vm create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vm-zone1 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --zone 1 \                     # Pin to Availability Zone 1
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --public-ip-sku Standard       # Standard Public IP required for zone support

# Verify the VM's zone placement
az vm show \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vm-zone1 \
  --query "{Name:name, Zone:zones[0], Location:location, Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" \
  --output json
# Expected: "Zone": "1"

# Deploy a second VM pinned to Zone 2 (for HA across zones)
az vm create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vm-zone2 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --zone 2 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --public-ip-sku Standard

After deploying app-vm-zone1 and app-vm-zone2, a zone failure affecting Zone 1 would take down app-vm-zone1 but leave app-vm-zone2 running. A load balancer in front of both VMs would automatically stop sending traffic to the failed zone (zone-aware health probing).

Zone-redundant services are the easier path to zone-level resilience — you configure the redundancy option once, and the platform manages placement and failover automatically. No runbooks, no manual intervention during a zone outage.
Use zone-redundant Standard Public IP or omit --zone on the IP
--zones 1 2 3 in az aks create / nodepool add
Azure SQL Database (General Purpose / Business Critical)
--zone-redundant true in az sql db create
Azure Application Gateway v2 / WAF v2

# Zone-Redundant Storage: one synchronous copy per AZ (3 copies total)
az storage account create \
  --name prodstoragezrs \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS \           # Zone-Redundant Storage
  --kind StorageV2

# Zone-Redundant Azure SQL Database (Business Critical tier)
az sql db create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --server prod-sql-server \
  --name appdb \
  --tier BusinessCritical \      # General Purpose also supports ZR
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 4 \
  --zone-redundant true          # Spreads across 3 AZs automatically

# Zone-Redundant AKS cluster (nodes spread across AZs)
az aks create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name prod-cluster \
  --node-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \                # One node initially per zone
  --node-vm-size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler \
  --min-count 3 \
  --max-count 9

# Zone-Redundant Azure Cache for Redis (Premium tier)
az redis create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name prod-redis \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium \
  --vm-size P1 \
  --zone-redundant \             # Spreads Redis shards across AZs
  --zones 1 2 3
Zone-Aware Architecture: Combining Zonal and Zone-Redundant
Most production architectures combine both models: zone-redundant for managed services and ingress, zonal for stateful compute nodes that require explicit placement control.

# Architecture: 3-zone AKS cluster with zone-redundant storage and SQL

# 1. Zone-redundant AKS system node pool
az aks create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name prod-aks \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --node-count 3 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_D4s_v5

# 2. Add a user node pool for stateful workloads (zonal — explicit control)
#    Three pools, one per zone, for zone-affinity with persistent volumes
for ZONE in 1 2 3; do
  az aks nodepool add \
    --cluster-name prod-aks \
    --resource-group prod-rg \
    --name "stateful${ZONE}" \
    --node-count 2 \
    --zones "$ZONE" \            # Each pool pinned to one zone
    --node-vm-size Standard_D8s_v5 \
    --node-taints "dedicated=stateful:NoSchedule" \
    --labels "zone-id=zone${ZONE}"
done

# 3. Zone-redundant storage for shared artifacts
az storage account create \
  --name prodaksartifacts \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS

# 4. Zone-redundant Azure SQL for the application database
az sql server create \
  --name prod-sql \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "Str0ngPas\$!"

az sql db create \
  --server prod-sql \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name appdb \
  --tier GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 --capacity 4 \
  --zone-redundant true

In this pattern, the Kubernetes control plane and system pods are zone-redundant (platform-managed). Stateful pods that need persistent volumes are scheduled to zone-specific node pools so that the pod and its storage are always in the same zone. Zone failure removes one of three stateful node pools; the other two continue serving.

Premium SSD, Ultra Disk, and Premium SSD v2 managed disks are zonal resources — they exist in exactly one AZ. When you attach a managed disk to a VM, both must be in the same zone. Azure enforces this at the control plane: attaching a Zone 1 disk to a Zone 2 VM fails immediately.

# Create a zonal Premium SSD managed disk (Zone 2)
az disk create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name data-disk-zone2 \
  --location eastus \
  --zone 2 \                    # Disk pinned to Zone 2
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --size-gb 512

# Attach to a VM that is ALSO in Zone 2 (fails if VM is in different zone)
az vm disk attach \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --vm-name app-vm-zone2 \      # Must be in Zone 2
  --name data-disk-zone2

# Verify disk zone placement
az disk show \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name data-disk-zone2 \
  --query "{Name:name, Zone:zones[0], SKU:sku.name, SizeGB:diskSizeGb}" \
  --output json

# Snapshot a zonal disk (snapshots are not zonal — they are stored regionally)
az snapshot create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name data-disk-zone2-snap \
  --source data-disk-zone2      # Snapshot can be used to restore in any zone

The zone affinity constraint means that if you need to move a VM from Zone 2 to Zone 3 (e.g., Zone 2 is experiencing a prolonged outage), you must: take a snapshot of the managed disk, create a new disk from the snapshot in Zone 3, and deploy a new VM in Zone 3 from the snapshot disk. Azure does not support live migration of zonal managed disks between zones.

Ultra Disk (for the highest IOPS and lowest latency storage) is always zonal — it can only be provisioned in regions that support AZs, and it must be pinned to a specific zone. There is no zone-redundant option for Ultra Disk because its synchronous write model does not support cross-zone replication at the required throughput.

# Check if Ultra Disk is supported in a specific zone of the target region
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --zone 1 \
  --resource-type disks \
  --query "[?name=='UltraSSD_LRS'].{Zone:locationInfo[0].zones[0], Restrictions:restrictions}" \
  --output json

# Create an Ultra Disk in Zone 1 (East US)
az disk create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name ultra-disk-zone1 \
  --location eastus \
  --zone 1 \
  --sku UltraSSD_LRS \
  --size-gb 256 \
  --disk-iops-read-write 10000 \   # Configurable up to 160,000 IOPS
  --disk-mbps-read-write 400        # Configurable up to 2,000 MB/s

# VM must be in Zone 1 and must have Ultra Disk enabled
az vm create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name ultra-vm-zone1 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --zone 1 \
  --ultra-ssd-enabled true \       # Enable Ultra Disk support on the VM
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

Some services are non-zonal — the platform places them within the region without AZ assignment. This is common for PaaS services that pre-date AZ support, or for SKUs where zone-redundancy has not been implemented.
Deploy multiple instances and use deployment slots; upgrade to Premium v3 for ZR support
Consumption plan is serverless — platform handles resilience; use Premium plan for zone control
Upgrade to General Purpose vCore or Business Critical for zone-redundant HA
Single datacenter within a zone (or no zone)
Use ZRS disk (Preview) or ensure VM is in a specific zone
ACI does not support AZ pinning; use AKS for zone-aware container orchestration

Non-zonal deployments in AZ-capable regions are still protected against single-server hardware failures by the platform's fault domain separation. They are not, however, protected against a full AZ failure (power, cooling, or networking outage affecting all datacentres in one zone). If your SLA requires zone-level resilience, migrate to a zone-redundant or multi-zonal architecture.

# List all VM SKUs that support zone deployment in East US
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query "[?not_null(locationInfo[0].zones) && length(locationInfo[0].zones) > '0'].{SKU:name, Zones:locationInfo[0].zones | join(', ', @)}" \
  --output table | head -25

# Find VM SKUs that DON'T support zones (non-zonal VMs)
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --query "[?length(locationInfo[0].zones) == '0' || !not_null(locationInfo[0].zones)].name" \
  --output tsv | head -15

# Check if an existing VM was deployed to a zone
az vm show \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vm-01 \
  --query "{Zone:zones, Location:location}" \
  --output json
# "zones": ["1"] means zonal; "zones": [] means non-zonal

# List all VMs in a resource group with their zone placement
az vm list \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "[].{Name:name, Zone:zones[0], Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" \
  --output table
VMSS Zone Spreading vs Zone Pinning
Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS) support two zone models depending on how you specify zones at creation:

# Zone-spread VMSS: instances distributed across zones 1, 2, and 3
# This is the recommended approach for stateless workloads
az vmss create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vmss-spread \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --instance-count 6 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \               # Platform distributes 6 instances across 3 zones (2 per zone)
  --orchestration-mode Flexible \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Zone-pinned VMSS: all instances in Zone 1 only
# Use this only when the workload has strict zone-affinity requirements
az vmss create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name db-vmss-zone1 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D8s_v5 \
  --instance-count 3 \
  --zones 1 \                   # All instances in Zone 1 only
  --orchestration-mode Flexible

# Check instance distribution across zones
az vmss list-instances \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-vmss-spread \
  --query "[].{InstanceID:instanceId, Zone:zones[0]}" \
  --output table

Zone-spread VMSS with --zones 1 2 3 distributes instances evenly across zones. If Zone 1 fails, the instances in Zones 2 and 3 continue serving; autoscale will add replacement instances only to the surviving zones until Zone 1 recovers.

For a multi-zone deployment to survive a zone failure, the load balancer in front of the fleet must be zone-aware — it must detect zone health and stop sending traffic to unhealthy zones automatically. Azure Standard Load Balancer (SLB) provides this capability through zone-redundant frontend IPs combined with per-zone health probes.

# Create a zone-redundant Standard Load Balancer
# The frontend IP spans all three zones — if one zone fails, 
# the LB continues routing from the surviving zones

# Create a zone-redundant public IP (no --zone flag = zone-redundant)
az network public-ip create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name lb-pip \
  --sku Standard \
  --zone 1 2 3               # Zone-redundant IP

# Create the load balancer
az network lb create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name app-lb \
  --sku Standard \
  --public-ip-address lb-pip \
  --frontend-ip-name app-frontend \
  --backend-pool-name app-backend

# Add health probe (probes each VM independently)
az network lb probe create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --lb-name app-lb \
  --name health-probe \
  --protocol Http \
  --port 80 \
  --path /health

# Load-balancing rule
az network lb rule create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --lb-name app-lb \
  --name http-rule \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --frontend-port 80 \
  --backend-port 80 \
  --frontend-ip-name app-frontend \
  --backend-pool-name app-backend \
  --probe-name health-probe

# Add VMs from all three zones to the backend pool
for VM in app-vm-zone1 app-vm-zone2 app-vm-zone3; do
  NIC_ID=$(az vm show --resource-group prod-rg --name "$VM" --query "networkProfile.networkInterfaces[0].id" -o tsv)
  az network nic ip-config address-pool add \
    --resource-group prod-rg \
    --nic-name "$(basename $NIC_ID)" \
    --ip-config-name ipconfig1 \
    --lb-name app-lb \
    --address-pool app-backend
done

When Zone 2 fails, the Standard Load Balancer's health probes detect that app-vm-zone2's /health endpoint is unreachable and stop routing to it within 10–30 seconds. Traffic automatically redistributes to app-vm-zone1 and app-vm-zone3 with no DNS change, no manual failover, and no downtime beyond the probe interval.
Zone Pricing: Is There an Extra Cost for Zones?
Deploying to Availability Zones does not carry a zone surcharge — you pay the same price for a Standard_D4s_v5 VM whether it is in Zone 1, Zone 2, or Zone 3. However, there are two indirect costs:

traffic between resources in different zones within the same region is subject to a bandwidth charge. As of 2024, intra-region cross-zone traffic is charged at approximately $0.01/GB. For most application tiers, this is negligible — but for high-throughput data pipelines that move hundreds of terabytes between zones, it can add up.

to achieve zone resilience, you typically need at least 3 instances (one per zone) where 1 or 2 would have sufficed for capacity alone. This is the intentional cost of resilience — you provision for peak load in the worst case of losing one zone, not just the baseline.

# Estimate cross-zone bandwidth cost
# Assume 3-zone AKS cluster with inter-pod traffic across zones
# 500 GB/month cross-zone traffic at $0.01/GB = $5/month — typically negligible

# Query actual cross-zone bandwidth usage (requires Network Watcher / metrics)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/prod-vnet \
  --metric "BandwidthConsumedPerFlowCount" \
  --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-01-31T23:59:59Z \
  --interval PT1H \
  --aggregation Total \
  --output table

# Identify high cross-zone traffic using Network Watcher flow logs
# (enables per-flow traffic analysis including AZ-to-AZ flows)
az network watcher flow-log create \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --name prod-flow-log \
  --nsg prod-nsg \
  --storage-account prodflowlogstorage \
  --enabled true \
  --format JSON \
  --log-version 2
ARM Template and Bicep: Expressing Zone Configuration
Infrastructure-as-code for zone-aware architectures must declare zones explicitly. Both ARM templates and Bicep express zones as an array property on the resource.

A zone-aware architecture that has never been tested for zone failure provides false assurance. Azure does not provide a native 'zone fault injection' service, but several approaches can validate zone resilience:

Intentional zone drain: cordon and drain all nodes in one AKS zone, or deallocate all VMs in one zone in a VMSS. Verify that traffic reroutes and that no data is lost. Re-enable the zone and verify recovery.

Health probe manipulation: return HTTP 503 from the /health endpoint on all instances in one zone, simulating a zone-wide application failure. Verify that the load balancer correctly routes to surviving zones.

Azure Chaos Studio: Microsoft's managed chaos engineering service supports zone-level fault injection experiments — shut down VMs in a specific AZ, inject network latency between zones, or kill processes. Use it to run controlled zone failure experiments without manual VM manipulation.
Quarterly zone-drain drill: schedule a quarterly drill where the on-call team deliberately fails one zone in a staging environment identical to production. Document the RTO and any issues found.

Untested resilience is not resilience. A zone-redundant architecture that has never experienced a simulated zone failure may have silent assumptions that only surface during a real incident — late DNS propagation, improper health probe configuration, sticky sessions that don't survive zone failover, or persistence layers that aren't truly zone-redundant.

Azure Availability Zone services fall into three models: zonal (pinned to a specific zone via --zone flag), zone-redundant (platform distributes across all zones automatically), and non-zonal (no zone assignment). Zonal services give you explicit placement control — necessary for managed disks, Ultra Disks, and low-latency co-location — but require you to deliberately spread instances across zones to achieve HA. Zone-redundant services (ZRS storage, Standard Load Balancer, AKS multi-zone node pools, zone-redundant SQL Database) handle zone failure automatically without customer intervention. Non-zonal services provide no zone-level SLA; upgrade to a zone-capable SKU or redesign the architecture before targeting a zone-resilient SLA. The strongest architectures combine zone-redundant managed services with zonal VM/disk placement where explicit zone control is needed.
